questions.questionText: KOH mount of skin scraping from an annular scaly lesion shows septate branching hyphae. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Dermatophytosis (tinea — dermatophytes: Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton produce septate branching hyphae on KOH)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Septate branching hyphae on KOH = dermatophyte infection. Why correct: Dermatophytes (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton) infect keratinized tissue (skin, hair, nails). KOH mount: septate branching hyphae. Annular scaly plaques with central clearing and active raised border. Why A wrong: Candida: KOH shows pseudohyphae + budding yeast cells. Why C wrong: Pityriasis versicolor (Malassezia): 'spaghetti and meatballs' (short hyphae + round spores). Why D wrong: Sporotrichosis: dimorphic fungus, lymphocutaneous nodules along lymphatics (rose gardener's disease). Clinical relevance: Tinea types: capitis (scalp), corporis (body), pedis (foot — athlete's foot), cruris (groin — jock itch), unguium (nail = onychomycosis). Treatment: topical azoles for skin; oral terbinafine/griseofulvin for nail/hair. Exam trap: Griseofulvin = DOC for tinea capitis in children (oral; topical doesn't penetrate hair shaft). Memory aid: 'Dermatophytes = Definite septate hyphae on KOH.' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Fungal.
